behaviors.description: The content passed to the summary-generation step includes data retrieved from all three sources
behaviors.justification: This tests data aggregation logic: does the code collect from all three sources and pass the combined data to summary-gen? A Unit test with mocked data sources (metrics endpoint, issue tracker, deployment log) can verify the aggregation correctly includes all three inputs. You don't need real I/O—just verify the in-process aggregation logic. A mock that fails to be called or whose return value doesn't appear in the aggregated dict would catch missing sources.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the three data sources, call the data aggregation function, and assert the returned data structure contains fields from all three sources. Verify that if one source's mock is removed, the test fails.

behaviors.description: When one data source is unavailable, the report is produced with a `data unavailable` note for that section rather than aborting
behaviors.justification: This is error-handling logic: does the code catch failures gracefully? A Unit test with a mocked data source that raises an exception can verify the code doesn't crash and produces the expected fallback text. The in-process mock can be configured to simulate unavailability (raise exception, return 500, timeout). You don't need a real network failure—the error-handling path is what matters.
behaviors.test_description: Mock one data source to raise a connection error (or return a 503 response). Call the report-generation function and assert: (1) the function completes without raising, (2) the output contains a 'data unavailable' marker for that section, (3) the other two sections have real data.

behaviors.description: The narrative summary accurately represents the key values present in the input data
behaviors.justification: This requires validating LLM output accuracy. A Unit test with a mocked LLM that returns a fixed summary would always pass—it doesn't test if the *real* LLM produces accurate summaries. A real LLM call is non-deterministic: the same input might produce different summaries across runs, and summaries could be inaccurate (hallucinating numbers, misrepresenting priorities, etc.). You must call a real LLM (or a deterministic test model) and validate that key values from the input appear correctly in the output.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real summary-generation API with test input containing specific key metrics (e.g., '5 critical bugs fixed', 'deployment success rate 98%'). Verify the summary text contains or accurately paraphrases these values. Either use a deterministic test LLM or run multiple times to check consistency. For fuzzy validation, use an AI judge ('does this summary accurately reflect the input?').

behaviors.description: A complete report — from data collection through email delivery — reaches the configured recipient's inbox correctly
behaviors.justification: This is a full pipeline: data collection (from 3 sources) → aggregation → summary generation (LLM, non-deterministic) → email formatting → actual email delivery. Each stage has failure modes: (1) Integration test with mocks would miss email delivery failures (spam, misconfigured recipients, service down); (2) the summary generation depends on LLM output (Agentic), which can't be mocked without hiding inaccuracy; (3) real email delivery requires real SMTP/API calls (System). Testing 'reaches inbox correctly' requires orchestrating multiple real external services (LLM + email).
behaviors.test_description: Run the complete pipeline with a real LLM API and a test email account. Verify: (1) data is collected from all three sources, (2) summary is generated and contains accurate content, (3) the formatted email is sent via real SMTP/email API, (4) the test email account receives the message with correct subject/body/sender. This may require a dedicated test email address and cleanup between runs.

behaviors.description: When the email delivery service is unreachable, the generated report is saved to a local file and delivery is retried on the next scheduled run
behaviors.justification: This tests error handling + persistence logic: does the code save to disk and schedule a retry when email fails? An Integration test with a mocked email service (configured to fail) and a real or mocked filesystem can verify: (1) the code catches the email failure, (2) the report is persisted to a file, (3) retry logic is scheduled. You don't need a real unavailable email service—simulating the failure with a mock is sufficient to exercise the error path. The file I/O can be tested with real filesystem writes (Integration) or mocked file operations.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the email service to raise a connection error. Call the report-delivery function and assert: (1) no exception is raised, (2) a report file is written to the configured backup location, (3) the file contains the complete report data, (4) a retry is enqueued (verify via mocked scheduler or check retry queue). On a second invocation, mock the email service as healthy and verify it retrieves and resends the saved report.
